risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to each group based on a computer-generated randomization scheme." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated randomization method, which is a proper random sequence generation procedure and supports low risk of selection bias for this domain.

Quote: "Subjects were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to each group based on a computer-generated randomization scheme." Comment: The paper reports random sequence generation, but it does not describe any method used to conceal the allocation sequence from the person enrolling participants. No central allocation, opaque sealed envelopes, or other concealment mechanism is explicitly stated.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This study was an investigator-blinded, randomized, parallel group study... The investigator was unaware of the type of medication being provided to the subjects... The study medications were dispensed by the study nurse." Comment: Only the investigator was blinded; the report does not state that participants or the personnel administering the treatment were blinded. Because the interventions were different oral contraceptive formulations and outcomes included subjective measures such as self-reported dysmenorrhea and acne assessments, knowledge of allocation by participants/personnel could plausibly influence outcomes.

Quote: "The investigator was unaware of the type of medication being provided to the subjects and assessed facial acne and dysmenorrhea while blinded in this way." Comment: The outcome assessor was the investigator, and the paper explicitly states that this investigator was blinded to treatment allocation during assessment. The main outcomes included acne lesion counts and clinician global assessment (investigator-rated) as well as patient-reported dysmenorrhea. Because the investigator who measured the clinical outcomes was blinded, and the objective lesion counts are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation, the risk of detection bias is low for the assessed outcomes.

Quote: "One woman in the EE/CMA group and one woman in the EE/DRSP group were lost to follow up." Comment: Attrition was minimal and balanced between groups (1/90 in each arm, about 1.1% per arm). The paper also states that "All randomized subjects who received at least one dose of the study medication and fulfilled the inclusion criteria were included in the intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis. All endpoint assessments were analyzed by ITT." With essentially complete follow-up and balanced loss to follow-up, attrition is unlikely to have biased the results.
